purpose_statements.purpose_statement: This file defines the data model, tests, semantic layer, metrics, and saved queries for order items in a business intelligence context, enabling revenue analysis and reporting by item, order, and time.

evidence.description: YAML structure containing models, unit_tests, semantic_models, metrics, and saved_queries sections defining business logic for order items

Multiple metric definitions including simple, ratio, derived, and cumulative revenue metrics

purpose_statements.purpose_statement: Converts monetary values from cents to dollars format for consistent financial reporting across different database platforms.

evidence.description: Macro definition shows conversion from cents to dollars with database-specific implementations for numeric formatting
Transformation dbt_model reads none and writes model.cents_to_dollars

purpose_statements.purpose_statement: This model creates a comprehensive orders mart by enriching staged order data with summarized order item metrics and customer ordering sequence information. It provides key business insights including order cost breakdowns, food/drink item categorization, and customer order history ranking.

evidence.description: order_items_summary CTE computes supply costs, item counts and food/drink categorizations
compute_booleans CTE joins orders with item summaries and derives business flags like is_food_order
customer_order_count CTE uses window function to rank customer orders by sequence
Transformation dbt_model reads model.stg_orders, model.order_items and writes model.orders

Import reference to orders
Import reference to order_items_summary
dbt reference stg_orders

purpose_statements.purpose_statement: This file defines a data mart for order analytics, containing detailed order information including financial totals, item types, and customer associations. It supports business reporting through semantic models and predefined metrics for order volume, revenue, and customer behavior analysis.

unit_tests section contains test cases validating order item boolean conversions

metrics section defines multiple business metrics including order totals, new customers, and item type counts

domains.description: Core business domain focused on order processing, revenue analysis, and customer purchasing behavior across the entire order lifecycle.
domains.reasoning: These files form a cohesive analytics pipeline for orders, customers, and order items. The marts (order_items, orders, customers) contain high business logic (0.8-0.9) for revenue analysis, customer lifetime value, and order enrichment. Supporting staging models (stg_orders, stg_order_items, stg_customers) provide standardized input data. All share key concepts like order processing, revenue metrics, customer behavior, and data transformation for analytics.

domains.description: Domain handling product catalog, supply chain data, inventory management, and cost tracking for business operations.
domains.reasoning: These files manage product and supply chain data with consistent patterns. Marts (products, supplies) serve as dimension tables for sales reporting and inventory management. Staging models (stg_products, stg_supplies) handle product data cleaning, price conversion, and supply cost tracking. They share concepts like product dimension, supply chain, inventory management, and cost conversion.

domains.description: Domain managing business location data, store operations, and geographic analysis for multi-site business management.
domains.reasoning: These files focus exclusively on location data with marts providing location analytics and financial metrics, while staging models transform raw store data. They share key concepts like location data, geographic analysis, and store operations management, forming a complete location data pipeline.

domains.description: Cross-cutting domain providing foundational data engineering utilities, project configuration, and data governance frameworks.
domains.reasoning: These are infrastructure and utility files that support all business domains. Macros provide currency conversion and schema management. Configuration files (dbt_project, __sources) define project structure and data sources. The time spine supports time-series analysis across domains. They share concepts like data governance, project configuration, environment management, and cross-domain utilities.

documentation_drift.explanation: Documentation sections don't match actual implementation details. The documentation mentions only three sections (ids, numerics, timestamps) but the code shows additional transformations not documented: currency conversion from cents to dollars and timestamp truncation.
stale_references: The documentation lacks references to currency conversion and timestamp truncation operations

evidence.description: Documentation sections (ids, numerics, timestamps) exist but don't describe the actual transformations: lines 22-24 show cents_to_dollars macro usage converting cent values to dollars, and line 27 shows date_trunc function truncating timestamps to daily granularity.

documentation_drift.explanation: The documentation headers in the code (ids, text, numerics, booleans) accurately describe the corresponding column transformations in the implementation.

evidence.description: Documentation headers match implementation: 'ids' section contains product_id (line 14), 'text' section contains product_name, product_type, product_description (lines 17-19), 'numerics' section contains product_price transformation (line 23), 'booleans' section contains is_food_item and is_drink_item flags (lines 26-28)

documentation_drift.explanation: The documentation comments accurately describe the corresponding YAML configuration sections, and the semantic model implementation matches the stated purpose of defining a supplies dimension table with supply-product grain.

evidence.description: Documentation comment '#The name of the semantic model.' correctly describes the 'name: supplies' field at line 3, and '#The name of the dbt model and schema' correctly describes 'model: ref('supplies')' at line 7.
Documentation comment '#Entities. These usually correspond to keys in the table.' correctly describes the entities section containing a primary entity 'supply' with expr 'supply_uuid'.

Documentation comment '#Dimensions. Either categorical or time...' correctly describes the dimensions section containing 5 categorical dimensions (supply_id, product_id, supply_name, supply_cost, is_perishable_supply).

Description 'Supplies dimension table. The grain of the table is one row per supply and product combination.' matches implementation with supply entity and product_id dimension, confirming supply-product grain.

documentation_drift.explanation: The documentation (comments within the code) accurately describes the implementation. Both the inline comments and the actual SQL transformations align perfectly.

evidence.description: Documentation comment 'ids' (line 13) correctly describes the 'id as customer_id' transformation (line 14). Documentation comment 'text' (line 16) correctly describes the 'name as customer_name' transformation (line 17).
